subsections.title: Collections Hierarchy
subsections.keyRule: Collection root: List (ordered, duplicates OK) → Set (no duplicates) → Queue (FIFO). Map is NOT a Collection. Key classes: ArrayList, LinkedList, HashSet, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet, HashMap, TreeMap, LinkedHashMap, ArrayDeque, PriorityQueue.

terms.what: List is an ordered Collection that allows duplicates. You can access any element by index. Maintains insertion order.
terms.why: When you need to store elements in order and might need the same value multiple times, List is your choice.
terms.how: List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add('a'); list.get(0); list.set(0,'b'); list.remove(0); list.size();
terms.where: Any sequence of data: shopping cart items, search results, steps in a process, rows from DB query.
terms.when: Use List when: order matters, duplicates are allowed, you need index-based access. Choose ArrayList for most cases.
terms.issue: Adding to or removing from the middle of a large ArrayList is slow — O(n) because all subsequent elements shift.
terms.fix: If you frequently insert/remove in the middle, use LinkedList. If you only add to the end and access by index, ArrayList is perfect.

star.s: Interview: which List to use for a shopping cart?
star.a: ArrayList — cart items have order, duplicates possible (2x same product), mostly add/iterate not insert- middle.

terms.what: Set is a Collection that stores unique elements only. Automatically rejects duplicates. No index access.
terms.why: When you need a collection where each element appears only once — unique tags, unique user IDs, visited pages.
terms.how: Set<String> set = new HashSet<>(); set.add('a'); set.add('a'); // second add ignored. set.contains('a'); // O(1) for HashSet.
terms.where: Deduplication, checking membership, storing unique values, permission sets, cache key tracking.
terms.when: Use HashSet for fastest operations (O(1)). LinkedHashSet if insertion order matters. TreeSet if sorted order needed.
terms.issue: Set requires correct equals() and hashCode() on your objects. Without them, duplicates sneak in.
terms.fix: Always override equals() and hashCode() in classes stored in Sets. Use Lombok @EqualsAndHashCode.

star.s: User permission list has duplicates causing incorrect access control.
star.a: Changed from List to HashSet<Permission>. Overrode equals/hashCode in Permission on permissionId.

terms.what: Map stores key-value pairs. Each key is unique. Values can be duplicates. Map is NOT a Collection — it has its own hierarchy.
terms.why: Fast lookup by key. Finding a value by key in HashMap is O(1) — constant time regardless of map size.
terms.how: Map<String,User> map = new HashMap<>(); map.put('alice', user); map.get('alice'); map.containsKey('alice'); map.entrySet() to iterate.
terms.where: Caches (userId→User), config properties, counting frequencies, grouping data, session storage.
terms.when: HashMap for fastest operations. LinkedHashMap to maintain insertion order. TreeMap for sorted key order. ConcurrentHashMap for thread safety.
terms.issue: HashMap is not thread-safe. Multiple threads reading/writing can corrupt the internal structure (infinite loop bug in Java 7).

subsections.title: ArrayList vs LinkedList
subsections.keyRule: ArrayList = dynamic array — O(1) get by index, O(n) insert/remove middle. LinkedList = doubly linked nodes — O(n) get by index, O(1) insert/remove at known position. For 95% of use cases, ArrayList wins. Only use LinkedList when you do heavy insert/remove at middle positions.

terms.what: ArrayList is a resizable array backed by an Object[]. Elements stored in contiguous memory. Index access is instant. Appending to end is fast.
terms.why: For most use cases, ArrayList is the right List. Fast iteration (CPU cache friendly — contiguous memory), fast get by index, fast add to end.
terms.how: Internally Object[] array. When full, creates a new array 1.5x larger and copies. Initial capacity 10. Set with new ArrayList<>(1000) to avoid re-sizing if you know size.
terms.where: Default List choice for almost everything. Spring query results, API response lists, batch processing collections.
terms.when: Use ArrayList when: you iterate frequently, access by index, add to end. Avoid when: you constantly insert or remove from the middle of large lists.
terms.issue: Inserting at position 0 in an ArrayList of 1 million elements copies 1 million references — very slow.
terms.fix: If heavy middle-insertion is needed, use LinkedList. But first ask: do you really need ordered middle insertion? Often a different data structure (queue, deque) is more appropriate.

star.s: Interview: I need to store 10,000 search results and display them paged. Which List?
star.a: ArrayList — indexed access for paging, results added once, iterated many times. LinkedList has no benefit here.

terms.what: LinkedList is a doubly-linked list — each node holds data + pointer to next and previous node. No contiguous memory. Fast insert/remove at known positions.
terms.why: For frequent insertion and deletion at arbitrary positions, LinkedList avoids the costly array shifting that ArrayList requires.
terms.how: Each Node object holds {data, prev, next}. get(500) must walk 500 nodes — O(n). add(node) at a known position just changes prev/next pointers — O(1). Implements both List and Deque.
terms.where: Queue/Deque implementations, situations requiring fast insert/remove at head or tail. Undo/redo history where you add/remove at ends.
terms.when: Use LinkedList when: you frequently add/remove at head or tail, or when you have a reference to the position. Rarely better than ArrayList in practice.
terms.issue: LinkedList is NOT cache-friendly — nodes scattered in memory. Iteration is slower than ArrayList even though O(n) for both. Memory overhead: each node stores 3 references.
terms.fix: Benchmark before choosing LinkedList over ArrayList. Often ArrayDeque is better for queue use cases. LinkedList's advantages only show in very specific heavy-mutation scenarios.

star.s: Task queue needs fast add to back and remove from front.
star.a: ArrayDeque is better than LinkedList for queue operations — less memory overhead, better cache performance.

terms.what: Iterator lets you traverse a Collection one element at a time safely. ListIterator additionally allows bidirectional traversal and modification during iteration.
java.util.Collections is a utility class with static methods for sorting, searching, shuffling, reversing, and making Collections thread-safe or unmodifiable.
terms.why: Provides common operations without reimplementing. Collections.sort(), Collections.unmodifiableList(), Collections.synchronizedList() are used everywhere.
terms.how: Collections.sort(list); Collections.sort(list, comparator); Collections.reverse(list); Collections.shuffle(list); Collections.unmodifiableList(list) — returns read-only view.
terms.where: Sorting before display. Creating read-only snapshots. Returning safe views from public APIs. Converting single-thread collections to thread-safe wrappers.
terms.when: Use Collections.sort() for simple sorts. Use list.sort() or Stream.sorted() in modern Java. Use unmodifiableList() for defensive copies in public APIs.
terms.issue: Collections.synchronizedList() makes individual calls thread-safe but composite operations (check-then-act) are NOT atomic.
terms.fix: For truly concurrent access, use CopyOnWriteArrayList (read-heavy) or manual synchronization on the whole block. For modern code, use java.util.concurrent classes.

star.s: Public API returns internal list — callers modify it and corrupt state.
star.a: Return Collections.unmodifiableList(internalList).
star.r: Callers get UnsupportedOperationException if they try to modify. State protected.

subsections.title: HashMap Internals
subsections.keyRule: HashMap uses hashCode() to find the bucket, equals() to find the exact key. Default capacity 16 buckets, load factor 0.75. When 75% full it doubles (resizes). Java 8+: when a bucket has 8+ entries it becomes a Red-Black Tree (O(log n) instead of O(n) for worst case).

terms.what: HashMap is an array of buckets. Each bucket is a linked list (or tree in Java 8+). Keys map to buckets via hashCode(). Duplicates in a bucket resolved by equals().
terms.why: Understanding internals helps you avoid performance pitfalls and debug 'key not found' bugs that come from broken equals/hashCode.
terms.how: put(key,val): hash = key.hashCode(); bucket = hash % capacity; walk bucket checking equals(); update or add. get(key): same two-step lookup. O(1) average, O(n) worst case (all in one bucket).
terms.where: Used everywhere as the most general-purpose key-value store. Cache implementations, config maps, frequency counters.
terms.when: Default Map choice. Not thread-safe — use ConcurrentHashMap for multi- threaded use. Use LinkedHashMap for ordered iteration.
terms.issue: All keys hashing to the same bucket = linked list grows = O(n) performance. Java 8 converts to Red-Black Tree at 8 entries — O(log n) worst case.

terms.what: LinkedHashMap is a HashMap that also maintains a doubly-linked list through all entries — preserving insertion order or access order.
terms.why: Regular HashMap has unpredictable iteration order. LinkedHashMap guarantees predictable order — great for caches where you need to track access order.
terms.how: new LinkedHashMap<>(16, 0.75f, true) — accessOrder=true makes it LRU order (last accessed moves to end). Override removeEldestEntry() for LRU cache behavior.
terms.where: LRU cache implementation. Preserving insertion order in JSON output. Any map where iteration order matters.
terms.when: Use LinkedHashMap when: you need predictable iteration order, or building an LRU cache. Otherwise HashMap is faster.
terms.issue: More memory than HashMap — extra prev/next pointers per entry. Slightly slower insertions.
terms.fix: Use computeIfAbsent(), putIfAbsent(), or compute() — these are atomic composite operations in ConcurrentHashMap.

star.s: Hit counter for URLs using HashMap crashes under load — corrupted state.
star.a: Changed to ConcurrentHashMap. Used map.merge(url, 1, Integer::sum) for atomic increment.

terms.what: TreeMap is a Map that stores entries sorted by key — natural order (Comparable) or custom Comparator. Backed by a Red-Black Tree.
terms.why: When you need keys in sorted order — alphabetical listing, sorted ID ranges, time- series data sorted by timestamp.
terms.how: TreeMap<String,Integer> map = new TreeMap<>(); — keys automatically sorted alphabetically. map.firstKey(), map.lastKey(), map.subMap(from, to) — range operations.
terms.where: Sorted config, range queries (find all events between time A and B), dictionary- style sorted indexes.
terms.when: Use when sorted key order is needed. All operations are O(log n) — slower than HashMap O(1) but provides ordering.
terms.issue: TreeMap requires keys to implement Comparable or a Comparator to be provided. Without it, ClassCastException at runtime.
terms.fix: Implement Comparable in your key class or provide Comparator: new TreeMap<>(Comparator.comparing(MyKey::getId)).

star.s: Need to list configuration properties in alphabetical order.
star.a: TreeMap — keys are property names, naturally alphabetically sorted. Iterate = sorted output.

subsections.title: Comparable vs Comparator
subsections.keyRule: Comparable = class defines its OWN natural ordering via compareTo() — baked in. Comparator = external class defines ordering — flexible, multiple orderings possible. Use Comparable for default sort. Use Comparator when you need different orderings for different scenarios.

terms.what: Comparable is an interface with one method: compareTo(T other). When a class implements Comparable, objects know how to compare themselves to each other — natural ordering.
terms.why: Allows objects to be sorted by Collections.sort() and stored in TreeSet/TreeMap without any extra code.
terms.how: class Employee implements Comparable<Employee> { public int compareTo(Employee other) { return this.salary.compareTo(other.salary); } } — sort by salary naturally.
terms.where: Domain objects with a clear natural order: Employee by ID, Product by price, Event by date.
terms.when: Implement Comparable when there is one obvious natural ordering. If multiple orderings are needed, use Comparator instead.
terms.issue: Natural ordering with Comparable is fixed — cannot be changed at call site. If sometimes sort by name, sometimes by salary, Comparable is not flexible enough.
terms.fix: Use Comparator for flexible, ad-hoc orderings. Use Comparable for the single 'default' ordering.

star.s: Employee objects need to sort by salary by default in TreeSet.
star.a: Implement Comparable<Employee> in Employee class. compareTo compares salary. TreeSet uses this automatically.

terms.what: Comparator is an external comparison strategy. It has compare(T a, T b) method. You pass it to sort methods at call time — flexible and decoupled.
terms.why: Allows multiple different orderings for the same class. Sort employees by salary here, by name there, by hire date elsewhere.

terms.what: Comparator.comparing().thenComparing() chains multiple comparison criteria. First criterion sorts primarily, second used when first is equal.
terms.why: Real data often needs multi-level sorting: sort by last name, then by first name if last names are equal.
terms.how: list.sort(Comparator.comparing(Employee::getDept).thenComparing(Employee::getName).thenCompari ngInt(Employee::getSalary));
terms.where: Any multi-column sort: reports, leaderboards, data exports.
terms.when: Use chaining instead of complex custom Comparator logic. Much more readable.
terms.issue: Forgetting .reversed() on just one part of the chain. Reversed() on the whole chain reverses all criteria.
terms.fix: Apply reversed() individually: Comparator.comparing(Employee::getSalary).reversed().thenComparing(Employee::getName) — salary descending, then name ascending.

star.s: Report needs: primary sort by region ascending, secondary by revenue descending.
star.a: Comparator.comparing(Row::getRegion).thenComparing(Comparator.comparingLong(Row::getRevenu e).reversed()).

subsections.title: Queue, Deque & PriorityQueue
subsections.keyRule: Queue = FIFO (add to back, remove from front). Deque = double-ended (add/remove both ends). PriorityQueue = sorted by priority (min or max heap). ArrayDeque is fastest Queue/Deque. PriorityQueue for scheduling, BFS uses Queue, DFS uses Stack (or Deque as stack).

terms.what: Queue is FIFO — first in, first out. ArrayDeque is the fastest Queue implementation in Java — backed by a resizable array.
terms.why: Task queues, print queues, BFS graph traversal, producer-consumer patterns all need FIFO ordering.
terms.how: Queue<String> q = new ArrayDeque<>(); q.offer('task1'); q.poll() removes and returns front. q.peek() sees front without removing. Use offer/poll/peek (return null) not add/remove/element (throw).
terms.where: BFS in graphs/trees, task processing, request queuing, event processing.
terms.when: Use ArrayDeque for Queue or Stack operations — faster than LinkedList, no null restriction of ArrayDeque vs LinkedList.
terms.issue: LinkedList used as Queue (common old code) has extra memory overhead per node. Slower than ArrayDeque.
terms.fix: Replace 'new LinkedList<>()' with 'new ArrayDeque<>()' as your Queue/Stack. Same Queue/Deque interface, better performance.

terms.what: PriorityQueue processes elements in priority order — not insertion order. Backed by a min-heap: smallest element comes out first by default.
terms.why: Job schedulers, Dijkstra's algorithm, event simulation, 'find top K elements' problems all need priority-based processing.
terms.how: PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(); pq.offer(5); pq.offer(1); pq.offer(3); pq.poll() → 1 (smallest first). For max-heap: new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()).
terms.where: Task scheduling (high priority first), Huffman encoding, 'top K' algorithms, Dijkstra's shortest path.
terms.when: Use when processing order depends on value/priority, not insertion time. Not a FIFO — do not use when insertion order matters.
terms.issue: PriorityQueue does NOT sort the entire array — only guarantees the root (peek/poll) is the minimum. Iterating a PriorityQueue does NOT give sorted order.
terms.fix: To iterate in sorted order: drain the queue with repeated poll() — each poll() gives next minimum. Or convert to sorted list first.

star.s: Interview: find the top 5 most expensive products from 1 million products.
star.a: Min-heap PriorityQueue of size 5. For each product: if pq.size()<5 add it; else if product.price > pq.peek().price, poll and add.
star.r: O(n log k) — much faster than sorting all.

terms.what: Deque (double-ended queue) can work as both a Queue and a Stack. Use Deque instead of java.util.Stack — Stack is synchronized and slow.
terms.why: java.util.Stack extends Vector which is synchronized — every operation locks even in single-threaded code. Deque gives stack operations without the overhead.
terms.how: Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>(); stack.push(1); stack.push(2); stack.pop() → 2 (LIFO). stack.peek() → top without removing.
terms.where: DFS traversal, undo/redo, expression parsing, call simulation, backtracking algorithms.
terms.when: Use ArrayDeque as Stack whenever you need LIFO. Never use java.util.Stack — it is a legacy class.
terms.issue: java.util.Stack has wrong inheritance (extends Vector) and is synchronized unnecessarily. Even single-threaded code pays lock overhead.
terms.fix: Replace 'new Stack<>()' with 'new ArrayDeque<>()'. Use push()/pop()/peek() — same method names.

star.s: Undo feature implemented using java.util.Stack — unnecessarily slow.
star.a: Changed to ArrayDeque as stack.
star.r: Same behavior, faster. No synchronization overhead.

subsections.title: Collection Interfaces Deep Dive
subsections.keyRule: Iterable → Collection → List/Set/Queue. Map is separate. SortedSet/SortedMap add natural ordering. NavigableSet/NavigableMap add range navigation. RandomAccess is a marker for O(1) index access. EnumSet is the fastest Set for enum values.

terms.what: Iterable is the root interface for all collections. Any class implementing Iterable can be used in a for-each loop. Has one method: iterator().
terms.why: for-each loop works on any Iterable — not just collections. You can make your own data structure for-each compatible by implementing Iterable.
terms.how: class MyRange implements Iterable<Integer> { public Iterator<Integer> iterator() { return new RangeIterator(); } } for(int i : new MyRange(1,10)) {...}
terms.where: All Java collections. Custom data structures. Stream sources.

terms.what: SortedSet extends Set with natural ordering (first(), last(), subSet(), headSet(), tailSet()). NavigableSet extends SortedSet with additional navigation: lower(), higher(), floor(), ceiling().
terms.why: When you need a set with sorted elements and range queries — sorted unique IDs, sorted timestamps, sorted names.
terms.how: NavigableSet<Integer> set = new TreeSet<>(); set.add(5); set.add(1); set.add(3); set.headSet(3) → [1] (less than 3). set.tailSet(3) → [3,5]. set.floor(4) → 3.
terms.where: TreeSet implements NavigableSet. Range queries, sliding window problems, ordered membership tests.
terms.when: Use TreeSet when you need sorted unique elements. Use subSet/headSet/tailSet for range queries.
terms.issue: TreeSet requires elements to be Comparable or a Comparator provided — otherwise ClassCastException.
terms.fix: Implement Comparable in element class or new TreeSet<>(comparator).

star.s: Need to find all events between two timestamps efficiently.
star.a: TreeSet of events. subSet(start, end) = O(log n) range query.

terms.what: EnumSet is a high-performance Set implementation for enum values. Internally uses a bit vector — each bit represents one enum constant.
terms.why: Extremely fast and memory efficient for enum-based sets. All operations O(1). Much faster than HashSet<MyEnum>.
terms.how: EnumSet<Day> weekdays = EnumSet.of(Day.MON, Day.TUE, Day.WED, Day.THU, Day.FRI); EnumSet<Day> weekend = EnumSet.complementOf(weekdays);
terms.where: Permission sets, feature flags, day-of-week sets, state machines with enum states.
terms.when: Whenever you use a Set of enum values. Always prefer EnumSet over HashSet<MyEnum>.
terms.issue: EnumSet only works with enum types — cannot use for non-enum values.
terms.fix: Use EnumSet for all enum-based sets. Keep enum definition stable — adding new values is fine, removing is breaking.

star.s: Cache needs to find entries with keys in a date range.
star.a: TreeMap keyed by date. subMap(startDate, endDate) returns only the needed entries.

terms.what: RandomAccess is a marker interface (no methods) that signals a List supports fast O(1) index-based access. ArrayList implements it. LinkedList does not.
terms.why: Algorithms can check instanceof RandomAccess and use index-based loops for ArrayList, iterator-based for LinkedList — optimal performance.
terms.how: if (list instanceof RandomAccess) { for (int i=0; i<list.size(); i++) { process(list.get(i)); } } else { for(T item : list) { process(item); } }
terms.where: Collections.sort() and binarySearch() check RandomAccess. Binary search uses index access for RandomAccess lists.
terms.when: Check RandomAccess in generic algorithm code when behavior should differ for index-based vs sequential lists.
terms.issue: Using get(i) in a loop on a LinkedList — O(n) per access — is O(n²) total.
terms.fix: Use iterator or for-each for LinkedList. Only use index loop on RandomAccess lists.

star.s: Generic algorithm using get(i) loop is slow for large LinkedLists.
star.a: Added RandomAccess check. LinkedList uses iterator path.
star.r: O(n) performance for all list types.

subsections.title: HashMap Advanced
subsections.keyRule: HashMap allows one null key (stored in bucket 0) and multiple null values. Hashtable is the legacy synchronized version — never use in new code. TreeSet.treeify happens at 8+ entries in one bucket to maintain O(log n) worst case.

terms.what: HashMap allows exactly ONE null key. The null key always goes to bucket 0. Multiple null values are allowed for different keys.
terms.why: Sometimes a null key has meaning — 'unknown category' mapped to some value. HashMap explicitly supports this.
terms.how: Map<String,Integer> map = new HashMap<>(); map.put(null, 42); map.get(null) → 42. map.put(null, 99) → overwrites. Only one null key.
terms.where: Optional category-to-value mapping. Default/fallback entries. Config maps with possible missing keys.
terms.when: Use null key when null has a meaningful semantic. Otherwise avoid null keys — can cause confusion.
terms.issue: TreeMap does NOT allow null key — throws NullPointerException. ConcurrentHashMap does NOT allow null key or null value.
terms.fix: Know which Map allows null: HashMap (null key/value OK), LinkedHashMap (OK), TreeMap (null key NO), ConcurrentHashMap (null key/value NO), Hashtable (null key/value NO).

star.s: Migrating from HashMap to ConcurrentHashMap — NPE appears.
star.a: Code used null key in HashMap. ConcurrentHashMap rejects it. Replaced null key with a sentinel string 'UNKNOWN'.

terms.what: Hashtable is the legacy Java 1.0 thread-safe Map. Every method is synchronized. HashMap is the modern non-synchronized version. Hashtable is obsolete — never use in new code.
terms.why: Hashtable's whole-map synchronization is a performance bottleneck. ConcurrentHashMap gives thread safety with much better concurrency.
terms.how: Hashtable<String,String> ht = new Hashtable<>(); — every get/put locks the entire table. HashMap = no synchronization. ConcurrentHashMap = bucket-level locking.
terms.where: Legacy codebases use Hashtable. All new code should use HashMap (single thread) or ConcurrentHashMap (multi thread).

terms.what: When a single HashMap bucket accumulates 8 or more entries, the linked list in that bucket is converted to a Red-Black Tree. This is treeification.
terms.why: Worst-case LinkedList bucket = O(n) search. With treeification, worst case becomes O(log n) — much better for pathological hash distributions.
terms.how: Static final int TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8. When entries in one bucket reach 8, Java converts that bucket's linked list to a balanced tree. Untreeify at 6 when elements removed.
terms.where: Automatic inside HashMap, LinkedHashMap, ConcurrentHashMap. Invisible to users.
terms.when: You don't control this — it's automatic. But you should ensure good hashCode() distribution to prevent buckets from growing large.
terms.issue: All keys with same hashCode() go to same bucket — attack known as Hash DoS. Treeify helps but O(log n) is still worse than O(1).
terms.fix: Use good hashCode() distribution. In security-sensitive contexts, randomize hashCode with string-hash seeds (Java does this for String since Java 7).

star.s: Interview: what happens when HashMap bucket grows large?
star.a: Java 8 treeifies at 8 entries — linked list becomes Red-Black Tree. Worst case O(n) → O(log n).

subsections.title: Blocking Queue Types
subsections.keyRule: BlockingQueue types: ArrayBlockingQueue (bounded, array-backed), LinkedBlockingQueue (optionally bounded, linked), PriorityBlockingQueue (priority-ordered), SynchronousQueue (direct handoff, no buffer), DelayQueue (delayed elements). Choose based on: bounded vs unbounded, ordering, and handoff semantics.

terms.what: ArrayBlockingQueue is a bounded blocking queue backed by an array. Fixed capacity set at creation. put() blocks when full. take() blocks when empty.
terms.why: Bounded capacity prevents OOM — producers slow down when consumers cannot keep up (back-pressure).
terms.how: BlockingQueue<Task> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<>(1000); q.put(task); // blocks if full. Task t = q.take(); // blocks if empty.
terms.where: Thread pool task queues, bounded producer-consumer pipelines, rate limiting of background work.
terms.when: Use ArrayBlockingQueue when you need a hard limit on queue size to protect from memory exhaustion.
terms.issue: Setting capacity too small causes producers to block frequently. Too large = defeats the purpose of bounding.
terms.fix: Capacity = (average processing rate) × (acceptable burst time). Monitor queue depth in production.

star.s: File processor OOM — reader faster than writer.
star.a: ArrayBlockingQueue(500) between reader and writer threads. Reader blocks when queue full.

terms.what: PriorityBlockingQueue is an unbounded blocking queue that orders elements by priority (natural order or Comparator). Highest priority element dequeued first.
terms.why: When tasks have different urgency — high-priority tasks must be processed before low-priority ones.
terms.how: BlockingQueue<Task> q = new PriorityBlockingQueue<>(11, Comparator.comparing(Task::getPriority).reversed()); q.put(urgentTask); q.take() → most urgent.
terms.where: Task schedulers, job queues with SLA tiers, Dijkstra's algorithm, event simulation.
terms.when: Use when tasks have urgency levels and must be processed in priority order, not FIFO.
terms.issue: PriorityBlockingQueue is unbounded — can grow without limit and cause OOM under heavy load.
terms.fix: Add a capacity check before adding tasks, or use a semaphore to limit the total tasks in the system.

star.s: Support ticket system — urgent tickets buried behind hundreds of normal tickets.
star.a: PriorityBlockingQueue with urgency comparator.

terms.what: SynchronousQueue has NO buffer capacity — zero elements stored. A put() blocks until a consumer is ready to take(). Each put and take are paired (direct handoff).
terms.why: Guarantees that a producer hands off directly to a consumer. No buffering — both must be present simultaneously.
terms.how: SynchronousQueue<Task> q = new SynchronousQueue<>(); Producer: q.put(task) — BLOCKS until a consumer calls take(). Consumer: q.take() — BLOCKS until producer calls put().
terms.where: Executors.newCachedThreadPool() uses SynchronousQueue internally. Direct handoff between known producer-consumer pairs.
terms.when: Use when you want direct producer-to-consumer handoff with no buffering — guarantees the task is being worked on immediately.
terms.issue: If no consumer is available, producer blocks indefinitely. Under bursts, producers may block.
terms.fix: Ensure enough consumers. CachedThreadPool spawns new threads as needed when SynchronousQueue is full.

star.s: Interview: what queue does CachedThreadPool use?
star.a: SynchronousQueue — each submitted task is handed directly to a new or waiting thread. If no thread available, new thread spawned.

terms.what: DelayQueue holds elements that become available only after a specified delay. Elements must implement Delayed interface. take() blocks until the first element's delay expires.
terms.why: For scheduling: 'process this task in 5 minutes', retry logic with backoff, scheduled message delivery.
terms.how: class DelayedTask implements Delayed { long executeTime; long getDelay(TimeUnit unit) { return unit.convert(executeTime - System.currentTimeMillis(), MILLISECONDS); } }
terms.where: Retry queues with backoff, scheduled task execution, session expiry, rate limiting.
terms.when: Use when tasks need time-based scheduling without a full scheduling framework.
terms.issue: Heavy use of DelayQueue can waste CPU checking delays in a tight loop. Large numbers of elements with similar delays cause contention.
terms.fix: Use ScheduledExecutorService for simpler periodic and delayed tasks. Use DelayQueue only for dynamic per-element delay logic.

star.s: Retry failed HTTP calls with exponential backoff.
star.a: DelayQueue with retry tasks. Each failure re-adds with doubled delay.
star.r: Retries with correct backoff without external scheduler.

terms.term: offer() vs add() poll() vs remove()
terms.what: Queue has two sets of methods: throwing (add/remove/element — throw exception on failure) and non-throwing (offer/poll/peek — return false/null on failure). Always use offer/poll/peek.
terms.why: Throwing exceptions for expected conditions (full queue, empty queue) is bad practice. offer/poll return values you can check.
terms.how: queue.offer(item) → returns false if full. queue.add(item) → throws IllegalStateException if full. queue.poll() → returns null if empty. queue.remove() → throws NoSuchElementException if empty.
terms.where: All BlockingQueue usage. Always prefer offer/poll/peek for graceful handling.
terms.when: Use offer/poll/peek for regular Queue operations. Use put/take for blocking (BlockingQueue specific).
terms.issue: Using add() on a bounded queue and not catching IllegalStateException — uncaught exception crashes the thread.
terms.fix: Always use offer(). Check return value. Use put() for blocking behavior.

star.s: Code uses queue.add() on bounded queue — throws exception under load.
star.a: Changed to offer() with return value check. Log if rejected.

terms.what: Comparator.naturalOrder() returns a Comparator that sorts in natural order (Comparable). reverseOrder() returns one that reverses natural order. Both are pre- built — no lambda needed.
terms.why: Convenient shortcuts — no need to write (a,b) -> a.compareTo(b) for natural order.
terms.how: list.sort(Comparator.naturalOrder()); list.sort(Comparator.reverseOrder()); Set<String> set = new TreeSet<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()); — Z to A.
terms.where: Sorting collections, reverse-sorted TreeMap/TreeSet, combining with thenComparing.
terms.when: Use naturalOrder()/reverseOrder() instead of writing lambda comparators for standard orderings.
terms.issue: Using reverseOrder() on a TreeSet of custom objects without Comparable defined throws ClassCastException.
terms.fix: Ensure elements implement Comparable, or combine: Comparator.comparing(Employee::getSalary).reversed().

star.s: Need top-N list sorted descending by score.
star.a: PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()) for Integer scores.

terms.what: nullsFirst() wraps a Comparator so null elements sort before non-nulls. nullsLast() puts nulls after. Prevents NullPointerException when sorting lists with nulls.
terms.why: Sorting a list with null elements using a normal Comparator throws NullPointerException. nullsFirst/Last handles nulls safely.
terms.how: list.sort(Comparator.nullsFirst(Comparator.comparing(Employee::getName))); — employees with null names go first, rest sorted by name.
terms.where: Any sort that might have null fields: optional database columns, optional fields, config with missing values.
terms.when: Use when the collection might contain null elements or null fields and you don't want to pre-filter.
terms.issue: Forgetting nullsFirst/Last when nullable fields are sorted causes NPE in production when rare null data arrives.
terms.fix: Add nullsFirst()/nullsLast() as a defensive measure for any field that could be null. TERM MEANING

star.s: Sorting product list by optional description field — NPE in production.
star.a: Comparator.nullsLast(Comparator.comparing(Product::getDescription)).
star.r: No NPE. Nulls go to the end.
